body_txt: We all have our own hobbies, concerns, and distractions. For most of us, we have multiple of these, and other priorities to boot. On a typical day, we can be switching through activities and thoughts several times, perhaps even every few minutes, based on necessity and circumstance. Then there's this character. On the surface, this trope is pretty simple. The character must have a hyperfixation that dominates the bulk of their thoughts, actions, and screentime. This hyperfixation is consistent, not simply something that comes and goes. If their attention shifts to something else, that thing becomes their next hyperfixation. They can think of non-fixations, and often will if the plot forces them to, but these brief moments of distraction won't be enough to stop them from going back to their fixation as soon as the situation resolves... though sometimes, their fixation will be so strong that even the biggest threat may not be enough to switch their focus, leading to Skewed Priorities . In short, they're obsessive and fixated on a consistent basis, to the point of being a notable character trait that colors nearly everything they do and think. As a rule of thumb, if the character isn't consistently focused on this one thing to the point of outright rejecting all other subjects, it's not this trope. How this trope impacts narrative and characterization is largely dependent on what the obsession is. Some are light and comical, often being mundane tasks or pleasures; the character in this case is going to be seen as quirky, but endearing, with the obsession often turning into comic relief. Others are dark and serious, usually centered around a specific goal or belief that colors their behavior and creates drama. Sometimes this can even make the character into a threat, someone with such a single-minded devotion to their motives or ideals that they're willing to harm others or risk their own life to get what they want. The severity of the obsession correlates directly with the severity of the portrayal, and this trope can fit heroes and villains alike. Other characters are likely to take notice of this obsession; in fact, this is a common tactic to make the trope obvious for the audience. People will have to either navigate around or play along with the fixation to communicate with them properly. Their reactions can range from amusement to annoyance to frustration or even fear, depending on how dramatic it's meant to be. If they try to get in the way of this fixation, they'll learn that such a thing is impossible. If they share a similar enjoyment or even just humor it for a while, though, then they can pretty easily bond with this character — they will just need to be patient with their differing priorities and repetitive nature. At times, this trope is used to write an explicitly or implicitly neurodivergent character. Many disorders, especially Autism and OCD, have hyperfixations (or "special interests") as a symptom. While this trope is often more exaggerated than anything in real life, it touches upon a real experience many audience members can relate to. In addition, this trope can result in a character struggling with or ignoring social norms and cues, having difficulty making friends or communicating, or becoming distressed if separated from their interest for a long enough length of time — all of which can also add onto the coding, while also making the character "different" in a more sympathetic and likeable way, contrasting the other stereotypes of Idiot Savant and Attention Deficit... Ooh, Shiny! . In a more general sense, this trope can also be used for general "geeky" type characters, such as the Otaku , Loony Fan , Absent-Minded Professor and Genius Ditz , though such characters often like multiple things in the same "genre" (ex: all forms of chemistry) rather than specific and singular examples (ex: thermodynamics), so not every example would overlap. While hyperfixations themselves are Truth in Television , this trope is generally more extreme, and discussing real-life people would qualify as gossip. No Real Life Examples, Please! . Supertrope of The Unfettered , for when the character's obsessive goal drives them to do outright amoral or even evil things. Compare One-Track-Minded Artist ; it obviously overlaps, but the artist may not be obsessed with doing the art in the first place; they just have a specific subject matter they focus on when they do. This can also result in Skewed Priorities , Detrimental Determination , or a Hyperfixation-Induced Error . If everyone in the setting has the same obsession, see Serious Business . May overlap with Lawful Stupid if someone is focused on following a code or law before anything else, including common sense, and Black-and-White Insanity if one has an extremely specific view of what's good and bad that they refuse to deviate from, or even consider might not always apply in reality. Single-Issue Wonk , Fleeting Passionate Hobbies , and Obsessive Hobby Episode are all related, but apply to occasional interests or something they're able to downplay until the button is pressed.
